title: Entries open for Melbourne Royal Wine Awards 2026
summary:
The Melbourne Royal Wine Awards is now accepting entries for 2026, inviting winemakers from across the country to benchmark their wines alongside the nation’s best. Judging for the awards will take place at the Melbourne Showgrounds from 19 to 23 October, with entries assessed through a “rigorous blind-tasting process by expert judging panels comprising leading winemakers, wine professionals and industry associates”. Wines will be grouped regionally to enable like-for-like comparisons and assessed using the internationally recognised 100-point scoring system. Organisers explained that trophy winners will undergo multiple rounds of evaluation, including re-tasting and ranking through the Borda Count method, “ensuring only the most exceptional wines receive Australia's most respected wine accolades”.

The post Entries open for Melbourne Royal Wine Awards 2026 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The awards feature a suite of “highly regarded trophies”. Image supplied

The Melbourne Royal Wine Awards is now accepting entries for 2026, inviting winemakers from across the country to benchmark their wines alongside the nation’s best.

Judging for the awards will take place at the Melbourne Showgrounds from 19 to 23 October, with entries assessed through a “rigorous blind-tasting process by expert judging panels comprising leading winemakers, wine professionals and industry associates”.

Wines will be grouped regionally to enable like-for-like comparisons and assessed using the internationally recognised 100-point scoring system. Organisers explained that trophy winners will undergo multiple rounds of evaluation, including re-tasting and ranking through the Borda Count method, “ensuring only the most exceptional wines receive Australia’s most respected wine accolades”.

The awards feature a suite of “highly regarded trophies”, including the Jimmy Watson Memorial Trophy (affectionately known as ‘The Jimmy’) which celebrates the legacy of beloved Carlton wine merchant and wine bar owner Jimmy Watson.

Other “coveted” trophies include the François de Castella Trophy for Best Young White Wine, the Champion Victorian Winery Trophy, the Douglas Seabrook Single Vineyard Trophy and the Kym Ludvigsen Trophy for Viticultural Excellence, which recognises the grower or viticulture team behind the winning single vineyard wine.

The Melbourne Royal Wine Awards also celebrate some of the industry’s most influential figures through dedicated honours, including the Dr Tony Jordan Trophy for Best Sparkling, the James Halliday Trophy for Best Pinot Noir, the Trevor Mast Trophy for Best Shiraz and the Bill Chambers Trophy for Best Fortified Wine.

In total, more than 20 major trophies will be awarded, including Best Sparkling, Best Riesling, Best Chardonnay, Best Shiraz, Best Pinot Noir, Best Cabernet Sauvignon, Best Grenache, Best Organic or Biodynamic Wine and Best Design, alongside state-based trophies “recognising excellence in key varietals”.

Kirrily Waldhorn, Melbourne Royal senior manager food & beverage awards, said the awards provide a vital platform for Aussie winemakers to benchmark their wines against the very best.

“Beyond the opportunity to compete for the industry’s most prestigious honours, including the iconic Jimmy Watson Memorial Trophy, the awards celebrate excellence across every region and style, while recognising the innovation and craftsmanship driving Australian winemaking forward,” Waldhorn said.

Chair of judges, Marcus Satchell, is looking forward to seeing what this year brings for the awards.

“The standard of entries continues to be exceptionally high year on year, reflecting the depth and diversity of Australian winemaking and so I’m looking forward to seeing what 2026 brings. Our judging process is rigorous and considered, ensuring every wine is assessed fairly and consistently through blind tasting to identify true excellence across each category,” Satchell said.

While organisers say the 2026 awards submission stays relatively the same as previous years, notable updates include streamlined judging for Best Single Varietal White, Best Single Varietal Red and Best Red Blend, with classes no longer split by varietal and vintage, but by varietal only. The event hosts said this change will provide greater submission opportunities for exhibitors.

Meanwhile, the Design Award criteria has been refined following industry feedback to ensure “a simpler and more relevant assessment process with exhibitors also having the opportunity to provide additional product information to support judging”.

Organisers said the Melbourne Royal continues to invest in the future of Australian winemaking through the ‘Melbourne Royal Wine Awards Professional Development Scholarship’. Awarded annually to the winemaker behind the Jimmy Watson Memorial Trophy-winning wine, or an employee of that winery, the scholarship supports “professional development opportunities that help recipients further their careers and contribution to the Australian wine industry”.

Established in 1883 with only 13 entries, the awards now attract more than 2,300 entries annually.

More information can be found here.

The post Entries open for Melbourne Royal Wine Awards 2026 appeared first on Winetitles.

title: ‘Lost in the FoG’ festival to return to Barossa and make inaugural Sydney debut
summary:
‘Lost in the FoG’ festival to return to Barossa and make inaugural Sydney debut
Grenache festival ‘Lost in the FoG’ will return to Alkina Wine Estate in the Barossa Valley this August for its fifth year as well as make its inaugural Sydney debut in the same month. Lost in the FoG is set to bring “more than 20 of Australia’s most acclaimed Grenache producers for an immersive weekend celebrating one of the country’s most exciting red grape varieties”. Organisers say the festival offers guests “the opportunity to spend an entire day exploring the remarkable diversity of Australian Grenache while meeting the growers and winemakers shaping its future”. Alkina Wine Estate winemaker Amelia Nolan said Grenache is a variety that continues to surprise and intrigue.

The post ‘Lost in the FoG’ festival to return to Barossa and make inaugural Sydney debut appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Grenache festival ‘Lost in the FoG’ will return to Alkina Wine Estate in the Barossa Valley this August for its fifth year as well as make its inaugural Sydney debut in the same month.

Lost in the FoG is set to bring “more than 20 of Australia’s most acclaimed Grenache producers for an immersive weekend celebrating one of the country’s most exciting red grape varieties”.

Organisers say the festival offers guests “the opportunity to spend an entire day exploring the remarkable diversity of Australian Grenache while meeting the growers and winemakers shaping its future”.

Alkina Wine Estate winemaker Amelia Nolan said Grenache is a variety that continues to surprise and intrigue.

“Every producer brings something different to the conversation, and that sense of discovery is what makes Lost in the FoG so special.

“It is a rare opportunity for guests to spend time with the people behind the wines, ask questions, learn about different approaches and experience the incredible diversity of Australian Grenache all in one place,” Nolan said.

Lost in the FoG will share its passion for Grenache with the Eastern Seaboard for the first time, making its Sydney debut on 1 August at The Wine Bar at The International.

This event poster. Image supplied

Running from 2 pm to 5 pm in Sydney and 11 am to 4 pm on both days in Barossa, event hosts shared that Lost in the FoG “is a rare opportunity to compare styles, discover new favourites and explore why Grenache has become one of Australia’s most talked-about wine varieties”.

title: Independent ag machinery repairers to discuss ‘right to repair’ reforms
summary:
A roundtable bringing together independent agricultural machinery repairers will be held this week to discuss the proposed expansion of Australia’s ‘right to repair’ law in regard to farm machinery. The Australian Automotive Aftermarket Association (AAAA), Australia's national industry association representing independent mechanical repairers, will convene a 'National Agricultural Machinery Repairers Roundtable' on Wednesday. The roundtable will bring together a small group of independent agricultural machinery repairers from across Australia for a practical online discussion. Treasury officials responsible for the current right to repair review will attend the roundtable as observers to hear directly from independent repairers.

The post Independent ag machinery repairers to discuss ‘right to repair’ reforms appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
A roundtable bringing together independent agricultural machinery repairers will be held this week to discuss the proposed expansion of Australia’s ‘right to repair’ law in regard to farm machinery.

The Australian Automotive Aftermarket Association (AAAA), Australia’s national industry association representing independent mechanical repairers, will convene a ‘National Agricultural Machinery Repairers Roundtable’ on Wednesday.

The roundtable will bring together a small group of independent agricultural machinery repairers from across Australia for a practical online discussion. Treasury officials responsible for the current right to repair review will attend the roundtable as observers to hear directly from independent repairers.

The federal government is actively considering whether the Motor Vehicle Information Scheme (MVIS) should be expanded beyond passenger vehicles to include agricultural machinery.

The AAAA says that for the review to be informed by real-world experience, government needs to hear directly from the independent repair businesses that keep Australia’s farms operating. Independent agricultural machinery repairers provide an essential service across regional and rural Australia, yet their experience is often absent from formal policy discussions, according to the association.

“Repairers are busy. They’re regional. They’re focused on keeping farmers working, not participating in Canberra policy processes,” said Lesley Yates, director of government relations and advocacy at the AAAA.

“Unless government hears directly from the businesses doing this work every day, there is a real risk that any future right to repair framework will be designed around assumptions rather than real-world experience.”

Rather than formal presentations, the discussion will focus on practical examples of the barriers repairers encounter when servicing modern agricultural machinery.

Participants will discuss the machinery they service, the work they are prevented from undertaking because of restricted access to information, software and diagnostic functions, how improved access could expand services for local farming communities, the practical issues government should address if agricultural machinery is brought within Australia’s right to repair framework, and the vital role independent repairers play in keeping farmers and regional communities operating.

The discussion is expected to include real-world examples involving diagnostic software, scan tools, security codes, software updates, calibrations, telematics, GPS-enabled equipment, connected machinery platforms, parts access, parts pairing, dealer-only functions and the impacts these restrictions have on farmers through increased downtime, travel costs and reduced service options.

“Independent repairers already support Australia’s agricultural sector every day,” Yates said.

“Where they have the information and tools they need, they keep machinery operating quickly and close to the farm. Where access is restricted, farmers can face unnecessary delays, higher costs and longer travel distances simply because independent repairers are prevented from completing work that they are fully capable of undertaking.”

“The people designing these laws need to understand what is actually happening in workshops and on farms across regional Australia. The practical experience shared through this roundtable will help ensure any future right to repair framework works for farmers as well as repairers.”

title: Friday Feedback 17/07
summary:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further. Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry. Read our recent roundup, featuring reader responses to Coonawarra’s Bowen Estate celebrates 50th vintage milestone and to yesterday’s article by Edward Cavanagh Seeing ghosts.

The post Friday Feedback 17/07 appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
If you’ve been reading the news and wondering what your peers think of it, look no further.

Our Friday Feedback segment provides a platform for the voices of our readers, highlighting the thoughts, reflections and musings of members of the wine industry.

 

Read our recent roundup here:

 

Responding to Coonawarra’s Bowen Estate celebrates 50th vintage milestone

“Doug Bowen would get a fair few nominations for best bloke in the wine game. As Classic Wine Merchants in the early 80’s, we were Doug and Joy’s agents in SA and to put it simply, Bowen wine was easy to sell. Behind Dougie’s dry-as (and often funny-as) exterior was a smart and simple strategy to make very good, often best, Coonawarra wine and at modest prices. Not a bad approach to ensure you get to 50 vintages in this challenging caper. Well done!”

  • David Ridge

 

Responding to Seeing ghosts

“Impressive article and can’t disagree with any of Edward’s analysis and conclusions. We’re in a parlous state as an industry and the duopoly is a major part of that, not that they treat any of their suppliers any better.

“From a lived experience, Delatite sold twice as much wine through Dan’s when it had 4 stores in Melbourne than we did five years after it had gone national. We now only sell a small volume of our Deadman’s Hill Gewurz through them, and I don’t care whether they sell it or not.”

  • David Ritchie

title: Art Gallery of NSW responds to criticism over wine lists
summary:
Earlier this month, the Art Gallery of New South Wales and other state-owned institutions in NSW came under scrutiny for having more interstate wines on the menu than locally sourced wines. The controversy was brought to light when Orange-based wine advocate David Cumming visited the restaurant at the Art Galley of NSW and found only three NSW wines on the venue’s list. “It simply makes sense for institutions whose purpose is to tell the story of NSW to have wine lists that reflect it,” he told the Western Advocate. Cumming said on a post to LinkedIn that his research found other NSW government-owned venues serviced by Pearl Catering, such as The Mint, had “no apparent NSW representation”. His objective is for the government to build a clause into future lease agreements that publicly owned venues need to represent NSW wine producers, “even if only 50% of the lists”. A spokesperson for the Art Gallery of New South Wales shared exclusively with Daily Wine News that the venue has asked its supplier to consider more NSW wine in future menu planning.

The post Art Gallery of NSW responds to criticism over wine lists appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
Exterior view of the Art Gallery of New South Wales. Image credit Iwan Baan.

By Kyra Trussell

Earlier this month, the Art Gallery of New South Wales and other state-owned institutions in NSW came under scrutiny for having more interstate wines on the menu than locally sourced wines.

The controversy was brought to light when Orange-based wine advocate David Cumming said he visited a restaurant at the Art Galley of NSW and found only three NSW wines on the venue’s list. He said this makes for just 8.8% of the wine on offer (3 of 34).

“It simply makes sense for institutions whose purpose is to tell the story of NSW to have wine lists that reflect it,” he told the Western Advocate.

James Robson of Ross Hill Wines in Orange agreed, telling the outlet, “It’s pretty disgraceful, really.”

“You don’t go somewhere in Italy and drink Australian wine…they need to be celebrating us,” he continued.

Cumming said on a post to LinkedIn that his research found other NSW government-owned venues serviced by Pearl Catering, such as The Mint, had “no apparent NSW representation”.

His objective is for the government to build a clause into future lease agreements that publicly owned venues need to represent NSW wine producers, “even if only 50% of the lists”.

Cumming said he has raised the concerns at a political level through discussions with Orange representative Phil Donato MP and Bathurst-based Paul Toole MP.

Speaking exclusively to Daily Wine News, a spokesperson for the Art Gallery of New South Wales clarified that the gallery is serviced by The Fresh Collective.

“Currently, 87.1% of wines offered by the glass and bottle across the Art Gallery’s three licensed dining venues operated by The Fresh Collective – Crafted by Matt Moran, Mod Dining and the Gallery Café – are Australian-produced. Of these, 14.5% are sourced from New South Wales and 72.6% from other Australian states.”

Furthermore, the spokesperson shared that the venue has asked its supplier to consider more NSW wine in future menu planning.

“While NSW food and beverage producers are already well represented across the Art Gallery of New South Wales’ restaurants and hospitality venues, the Art Gallery welcomes further opportunities to support local producers and has asked The Fresh Collective to consider additional NSW wine offerings as part of future menu planning and seasonal menu reviews.”

Are you a Daily Wine News subscriber? If not, click here to join our mailing list. It’s free!

Have you got news to share? Contact journalist@winetitles.com.au for the chance to be featured.

The post Art Gallery of NSW responds to criticism over wine lists appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Newly refurbished cellar door opens in the Tamar Valley
summary:
Tasmanian wine estates Tamar Ridge and Pirie have unveiled a freshly refurbished cellar door in the Tamar Valley. Located 20 minutes north of Launceston, the cellar door has been designed “to reflect the quality and craftsmanship synonymous with both brands”. Completed in just eight weeks by local builder Zanetto, the brands say the transformation “introduces a refined contemporary interior”, including “a striking double-sided fireplace” and “bespoke Spiegelau crystal glassware”. Luxury portfolio manager Katherine Brown said the new cellar door reflects the evolution of both brands while remaining true to their Tasmanian roots.

The post Newly refurbished cellar door opens in the Tamar Valley appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The cellar door is now open daily from 10 am to 5 pm. Image supplied, credit Natasha Mulhall

Tasmanian wine estates Tamar Ridge and Pirie have unveiled a freshly refurbished cellar door in the Tamar Valley.

Located 20 minutes north of Launceston, the cellar door has been designed “to reflect the quality and craftsmanship synonymous with both brands”.

Completed in just eight weeks by local builder Zanetto, the brands say the transformation “introduces a refined contemporary interior”, including “a striking double-sided fireplace” and “bespoke Spiegelau crystal glassware”.

Luxury portfolio manager Katherine Brown said the new cellar door reflects the evolution of both brands while remaining true to their Tasmanian roots.

“It’s incredibly exciting to welcome guests to a true brand home for Tamar Ridge and Pirie that reflects the quality, prestige and character of our wines.

“Set high above the Tamar River, the cellar door enjoys one of the region’s most spectacular outlooks, creating an extraordinary setting in which to experience our fine wines. Whether you’re relaxing beside the fireplace with a glass of Tamar Ridge Reserve Pinot Noir or enjoying a flute of Pirie Vintage sparkling overlooking the valley, we want every visit to feel memorable, considered and unmistakably Tasmanian,” said Brown.

The brands shared that the cellar door team will be hosting a collection of tasting experiences, such as the ‘Pirie Supreme Indulgence Experience’, which pairs Méthode Traditionnelle sparkling wines with Oscietra sturgeon caviar. It added that another highlight is the ‘Ultimate Expressions of Pinot Noir’ tasting, which showcases five of Tamar Ridge’s “most distinguished Pinot Noirs”, including limited Single Block releases.

The cellar door is now welcoming visitors daily from 10 am to 5 pm.

title: Seeing ghosts
summary:
The growth of supermarket-owned wine brands has been one of the most significant — and contentious — developments in the Australian wine sector over the past two decades. In the first of a series of articles examining market structure, competition policy and the future of the Australian wine sector, wine historian and commentator Edward Cavanagh examines the rise of private-label wines, the concentration of retail market power, and the policy decisions he argues helped shape today’s industry challenges.

The post Seeing ghosts appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
INDUSTRY ANALYSIS: How supermarket private labels, retail consolidation and competition policy reshaped Australia’s wine market

The growth of supermarket-owned wine brands has been one of the most significant — and contentious — developments in the Australian wine sector over the past two decades. In the first of a series of articles examining market structure, competition policy and the future of the Australian wine sector, wine historian and commentator Edward Cavanagh examines the rise of private-label wines, the concentration of retail market power, and the policy decisions he argues helped shape today’s industry challenges.

 

The recent announcement by Endeavour Group that it intends to sell off a number of its winery and vineyard assets around the country, while retaining the brands for in-house production, has ‘rattled the industry’, as the formidable Erin Leggat, CEO of McLaren Vale Grape Wine and Tourism Association, put it in on a LinkedIn post on 30th May. ‘We all know that for Endeavour this decision was just business’.

But, Leggat continued: ‘This industry isn’t about business, it’s about people, and particularly in times such as these we must never forget that’.

We should never forget, either, how and where this trend began, and why it has completely stuffed the Australian wine industry.

This article will attempt to remind readers of exactly that.

 

The rise of the duo

The power of Woolworths and Coles in Australia – which translates to the buying power of a duopsony as well as the selling power of a duopoly – is arguably the most important corporate development of the first quarter of the twenty-first century for the wine industry.

Having stitched up around 80 per cent of the retail grocery market share in Australia at the turn of the millennium, both of the supermarket giants then began to achieve the same kind of market dominance in the sphere of liquor retailing.

Acquisition of independent liquor chains – using the raw power of capital – was the commonest and bluntest method at the disposal of each of these supermarket empires.

For Woolworths, the first serious acquisition, which would become and remain their flagship, was Dan Murphy’s in 1999, which was followed by Tooheys Bros and Booze Brothers in 2000, the Super Cellar Group and Bailey & Bailey in 2003, and what was arguably most significant of all, the Australian Leisure and Hospitality Group, or ALH, from Foster’s, outbidding Coles, in 2004.

For Coles, it all started much earlier with Liquorland in 1981, which was followed by the buy-outs of Vintage Cellars in 1992, the Australian Liquor Group in 2001, and Theo’s in 2003.

By 2005, Woolworths could boast of 950 outlets nationally and 26% of the market share, whereas Coles could boast of 625 outlets and 19% respectively.1

Crucially, each had come into the possession of a considerable stockpile of liquor licences, which was, remarkably, achieved through a coordinated effort of state-level regulatory workarounds, and they incorporated these licences into their own separate development plans to entrench their footprints within and alongside established supermarkets in addition to those proposed in new suburban shopping centre developments.

It seems significant that the first major wine company to feel the brunt of this new and concentrated form of power was Southcorp when it was going through the earliest stages of a wine glut and cashflow problem. This, of course, is fitting in the narrative of the Australian wine industry: in a way, discounting its way out of trouble at the first sign of overproduction, and so soon after it declared itself to be the biggest and best premium wine company in Australia, one could be forgiven for saying that Southcorp not only drove itself, but also the wider industry, off a cliff.

But the story is a touch more complicated than that.

 

Supermarket superpowers

Coles (the owners of Liquorland, First Choice, Vintage Cellars, and others) and Woolworths (the owners of BWS, Dan Murphy’s, Cellarmasters and Langtons) only increased their strangleholds upon the domestic wine retail sector in the 2010s.

Between them, they had managed to acquire about half of this market by 2005. As early as 2009, however, Wine Australia estimated that the ‘supermarket share of the off-licence market’ was closer to 76%, although this figure presumably also included Metcash for licence-holding independent supermarket chain IGA, in addition to a small but growing number of Aldis. By 2015, though, it was reported to the Senate Inquiry that Coles-Wesfarmers and Woolworths had between them stitched up anywhere between 60% and 70% of the wine ‘retail market’, and we can work vaguely on that basis for the following analysis, noting only the following very important caveat: if this off-premise market left 30-40% in the hands of independent liquor retailers, online outlets and cellar doors, one has to take into account the value of the wine sold within this market share – since supermarket liquor stores sell most of their wines at the lowest price points, whereas boutique wine shops, online outlets and cellar doors sell most of their wines at the medium and top end of the premium price point. If my understanding here is correct, then the true scale of the total quantity of wines sold in Australia by Woolworths and Coles-Wesfarmers would be a much more intimidating and eye-watering figure to contemplate than 60-70%.

In any case, the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 was meant to prevent this kind of thing.

But it turns out that there was very little within this statute to prevent Coles-Wesfarmers and Woolworths from attaining even greater control of the market than they enjoyed even before that legislation took effect in 2011. This level of control allowed them to keep getting up to a number of their old tricks, including gouging, and passing on, wherever possible, most if not all of the marketing and logistical costs to wholesalers and winery businesses.

But it also allowed them room to develop a haunting new strategy that was particularly damaging for the industry.

The turning point in this story comes with the Woolworths acquisition of Dorrien Estate and Vinpac as part of the Cellarmasters takeover in 2011. Previous to this, Cellarmasters had originally been a takeover target by Fosters, before ultimately it had to be sold off amidst the catastrophe of that company’s divestment and disappearance from the wine industry. By 2011, Cellarmasters had found its way into the portfolio of a private equity firm, which is when Woolworths made its move. The acquisition went ahead, prompting the creation of Pinnacle Drinks as a subsidiary of Woolworths in 2012. Pinnacle Drinks then became the stable of a small but growing number of ghost labels: wines that were branded and bottled but mass-produced if not at Dorrien Estate then by contracted third parties or otherwise blended together from cheap bulk acquisitions resulting from desperate trading conditions in the market.

And Woolworths moved fast, buying up brand names and acquiring others from multinational conglomerates that were desperate for access to the Dan Murphy’s retail market and the ALH wholesale market.2

“Within a few years the supermarkets discovered that it was much cheaper just to get their in-house marketing teams to invent brands out of thin air and reserve space on the shelves for entirely new SKUs that looked authentic but actually weren’t.”

Some of these brands were acquired by Pinnacle and, emulating the strategy, Coles Liquor, in the same way that, for example, Mildara had acquired Yellowglen back in the day,3 or – more recently – in much the same way that Fosters and later Treasury Wine Estates acquired the brands that found their way into their portfolio, and ditto for the way Hardy and later Accolade (now Vinarchy) found itself shackled to an equally daunting catalogue of brands.

If this brand-buying spree of the supermarkets implies a degree of continuity with the evolutionary shape of these other large businesses within the sector, then any reasonable reflection upon the state of Vinarchy and Treasury Wine Estates as a result of their almost endless and increasingly difficult-to-move catalogues of SKUs in more recent times would persuasively suggest that continuity doesn’t always necessarily translate into profitability.

To look at this another way, the stark difference between these big commercial wine companies, and the supermarket chains, is the reality that Accolade and Treasury were never able to stitch up the retail market, whereas Woolworths and Coles did; indeed, so powerful had Woolworths and Coles become by the early 2010s that even Accolade and Treasury Wine Estates, in spite of their powerful stables of brands, were often forced into wholesale arrangements that squeezed their own margins of profit to the bare minimum. If nowadays it can be hard for some wine industry types to find much sympathy for the big corporate commercial wine businesses in Australia, one should never discount, if that is the right word, the fact that many of their financial woes clearly derive from the ways in which they have been shafted now by supermarkets for more than 20 years: a trend, we remember, that started way back when the major liquor retailers circled on the SKUs of a beleaguered Southcorp.

That might be a digression, but it is one that begs an interesting hypothetical question: would the fates of Accolade and Treasury in the 2010s have been any different if the wine retail sector had been regulated differently? Shiraz for thought.

Pinnacle Drinks was first to develop the strategy properly that Coles Liquor then copied, which involved acquiring brands for the purpose of sticking familiar labels onto bottles that could then be filled up with indistinct bulk purely to capitalise on a sense of nostalgia that animated several of their customers.

Within a few years the supermarkets discovered that it was much cheaper just to get their in-house marketing teams to invent brands out of thin air and reserve space on the shelves for entirely new SKUs that looked authentic but actually weren’t. Some of these brands controversially even borrowed names and iconography from already well-established independent wine brands.

Woolworths and its liquor arm, Pinnacle, then took this strategy up to another level when it started to buy actual wineries – some of them premium and much-beloved wineries. After poaching Andrew Wilsmore from the Winemakers’ Federation of Australia to make him cheerleader-in-chief for this new and unfolding side of the business model, Woolworths (and, its spin-off, Endeavour) also bought a few vineyards too.

Some of these acquisitions were successful and some of them weren’t, but the most important of all of them, by far, remained Dorrien Estate in the Barossa Valley.

This is not because Dorrien was as well-known as some of the others, and especially those that were subsequently picked up as part of the Endeavour shopping spree after 2019 (Cape Mentelle and Chapel Hill, for example, were in a different league, and eventually became part of another Endeavour subsidiary called Paragon Wine Estates). Rather, Dorrien Estate was important because it became the workhorse winery for over a hundred ghost brands and each of the many SKUs that were embalmed within these brands.

As already noted, Woolworths took ownership of Vinpac as part of the same Cellarmasters transaction, the significance of which cannot be missed: Vinpac had grown to become a powerful contract bottling, packaging and warehousing operation in the 1990s with two key locations: one just 5km away from Dorrien Estate, and the other situated in McLaren Vale.

And that’s when the private label operation really began to hum like a V8 Commodore.

Interviewed for The Shout in 2013, a spokesperson for Pinnacle Drinks, Brad Banducci (who would go onto become CEO of Woolworths in 2016), defended his company’s new strategy of vertical integration in an interesting kind of way:

“We think [these acquisitions were] essential because in order to grow own or exclusive brands, you do need to be a very disciplined brand builder. […] We’re about building brands, we’re not about building private label. There is a massive difference between those two things.”⁴

It should be unobvious to anyone today how it is possible to sustain that there really is ‘a massive difference between those two things’, as Banducci did then.

For its part, Coles Liquor leant into its own strategy effectively if much less aggressively.

It preferred to work at arm’s length from third-party winemaking outfits. Usually, this involved engaging bulk wine factories in places like Mildura, and working with a few blending ‘drinks’ operations in and around Melbourne, and buying directly from any winery across the country that brokers could force into accepting a dollar or two per litre.

The Woolworths Group still adopted the same techniques whenever they were able to seize the best opportunities from the broken bulk wine market, but Pinnacle Drinks within Woolworths had created an entirely different kind of operation for the company. This exposed Woolworths to greater risks and higher operating costs, but it also rewarded the company with economies of scale and higher profit margins, while, importantly, allowing the business to service third parties through contract winemaking at Dorrien and through packaging and bottling at Vinpac.

“When all of these bottles were given shelf space at the leading liquor outlets in Australia, that left much less space on the shelves for genuine brands.”

Woolworths then doubled down on this strategy when it bought Prowine in 2018, a contract wine bottling and packaging facility in Nuriootpa that had been in operation since 2004. This was a move calculated both to expand the capacity of Vinpac’s contract and in-house operations while knocking out one of Vinpac’s major competitors in South Australia. Whether or not it was the plan the whole time, the Prowine facility would ultimately be closed by Endeavour in 2025.

It is important to acknowledge that the ACCC looked into all of these Woolworths acquisitions and waved each one of them through, despite protests from the industry lobbying groups and earnest reporting by financial journalists at the time.

It is also important to acknowledge that the government explicitly knew that the WET rebate scheme was being manipulated by supermarkets in a way to place downward pressure on bulk wine values, even before the supermarkets themselves were able to prove that ownership of the brands and responsibility for production thereby often entitled them to access the rebates themselves.

But no legislative changes were delivered to prevent any of this activity.

Instead, what occurred, between 2012 and 2021, was an incredible surge in ghost labels. Coles developed a portfolio of more than 80 ghost labels, whereas Woolworths developed a portfolio of more than 200 ghost labels. When all of these bottles were given shelf space at the leading liquor outlets in Australia, that left much less space on the shelves for genuine brands. If one of the biggest problems in the Australian wine industry today is the overabundance of SKUs – the ratio of vineyard area to wine SKUs has completely blown out – then this is a policy problem in the area of consumer and competition law that should have been identified in the mid-2010s.

And, to some degree, albeit ultimately farcically, it was.

 

Why nothing has been done

The sphere of liquor retailing had long been bloodthirsty, of course. And this was especially the case in the 1980s, which is one of the reasons that led to the explosion of cellar doors in Australia. When a glut was healed by the band-aid of discounting, the producers got bruised, and the retailers turned nasty.

But the kind of power that had accrued to Woolworths and Coles in the 2000s was nothing like Australia had ever seen before.

And this conduct, while ostensibly legal, was there for everyone, including the Senate Economics References Committee, to see. In its inquiry into the ‘effectiveness of the Trade Practices Act 1974 in protecting small business’, which was established in June 2003 to report in March 2004, the retail liquor sector was singled out on numerous occasions for becoming an especially febrile environment for ‘creeping’ acquisitions by the ‘larger chains’.

Even the ACCC acknowledged and lamented this in its testimony to the Senate, while throwing up its hands, so perfectly as if by habit, to confess of its powerlessness to do anything about the problem.

With independent liquor groups strongly represented among the witnesses, this report was clear that the legislative threshold for ‘substantial degree of market power’ was too low and therefore ineffectual at constraining the anticompetitive activities of Coles and Woolworths in the national retail sector.

But nothing substantial was ever done, and the tightening up of the duopoly, and the related private label phenomenon – which was indirectly incentivised by the laws determining eligibility requirements for the wine equalisation tax (WET) rebate – only continued into the early 2010s.

It was not until 2015, when the Rural and Regional Affairs and Transport References Committee in the Senate delivered its terms of reference for a new standalone inquiry into the wine industry, that the issue came back to the attention of government.

Going through all of its submissions and testimonies, the committee was able to boil down its concerns to four major areas: the problems inherent to the WET rebate system; the consolidation of the wine retail sector; the operation of Wine Australia since its amalgamation; and the industry bodies generally but, in particular, the low uptake of their code of conduct since it was first delivered back in 2009. This report, which was endowed with an unusually large number of slightly dissenting opinions, was delivered to the parliament of the Turnbull government in February 2016.

It was in the committee’s consideration of ‘the power and influence of retailers of Australian wine in domestic and export markets’ that it had the chance to inspire real change, in the context of concurrent concerns about competition law more generally, to the legislative environment that had allowed for the supermarkets to take control of the market in the ways that they had.

When it came to supermarket ghost labels specifically, the committee recommended ‘that the Government amend labelling requirements so that wine labels must declare whether wine is produced by an entity owned or controlled by a major retailer’.

“We are in the position we are because of irrelevant and insufficiently robust legislation when it comes to anti-competitive behaviour in both the buying and the selling side of the Australian wine industry.”

Senator Xenophon made it clear in his imaginative dissenting opinion that, while he agreed with this recommendation, he was firm in the conviction that the issue of ‘private brands’ required considerably greater regulation beyond a labelling requirement. For Xenophon, the issue went to the heart of competition law, arguing that ‘we need to go further to allow small winemakers to grow and flourish’. This involved placing a ‘strong effects test in our competition laws […] to ensure abuses of market power do not occur’. Moreover, Xenophon urged here, which chimed with another of his proposals in the Senate, that new ‘divestiture powers’ be introduced into the law to penalise supermarkets through a ‘penalty in cases of abuse of market power’.

Senators Heffernan and Edwards, in their own equally interesting dissenting opinion, were more prosaic in their observation that ‘the consolidation of retail outlets to two large companies in the 12 years from mid-30% to now mid-70% requires further investigation to test if this has led to market failure’. And this, the Liberal pair were right to point out, was actually within the power of their house to trigger internally. Thusly they implored that ‘in accordance with Senate Standing Order 25(2)(a) the Economics Committee undertake an inquiry into the performance of the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission with respect to its role in the prevention of the misuse of market power by certain retailers in dealing with the wine industry’.

This throwaway remark in a dissenting opinion was, surprisingly, the most instrumental of all. For though none of the recommendations around liquor retailing above led to any legislative change, it took only two weeks after this report was delivered to parliament for the Economics Committee in the upper house to resolve upon a new and more specific inquiry into ‘anti-competitive conduct in the retail wine industry and ACCC’s role’. Launched on 26 February 2016, ‘the committee’s main focus is to determine whether there is anti-competitive behaviour in the industry and, if so, what role the ACCC has in promoting competition, and any impediments that are preventing the regulator from performing its function effectively’.

The chance had finally come for the government to own up to the shortcomings in the law, and the operation of the watchdogs, when it came to the retail market squeeze that the wine sector had been enduring for years, and central to this problem was the hoarding of ghost labels.

The committee invited submissions immediately, with a due date of 29 April, and determined to produce a report with recommendations by 30 September 2016.

But this committee was a flop.

For less than two weeks after all of the submissions had come in, Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull was advising the Governor-General to approve a double dissolution. This sent the country to an election, which forced the inquiry to lapse.

Now, Turnbull’s manoeuvring probably had nothing to do with any desire of his to protect the supermarket duopoly-duopsony and defend their large caches of private brands: he never went on the record to say any of that (although there is an interesting, if mostly irrelevant, dot-point on Turnbull’s extraordinary CV that states he did have some experience with buying wine brands for Allied Domecq).

The reason for taking such an extraordinary step was ostensibly due to a deadlock that had formed in the Senate around this time in the context of a doomed attempt to pass legislation designed to reintroduce a workplace regulator for the construction and building industry. Convinced that such a deadlock was unworkable – that he did not have the horses to trade his way out of the impasse – he decided instead to turn out the lights.

And that suited Coles and Woolworths just fine.

After all, they did most of their best work in the dark.

 

Where are we now?

Newly composed iterations of the Senate thereafter lost a lot of interest in the anti-competitive forces that were afflicting the wine retail market.

In more recent years, the ACCC too has become overwhelmingly more concerned with anti-competitive forces in the grape farming market.

Meanwhile, Australian Grape & Wine has consistently tried to ensure that wine remains in policy conversations through submissions to inquiries on national competition policy, supermarket pricing strategies, and the food and grocery code. These efforts have been valiant, but ultimately fruitless: frustratingly, recent governments have preferred to treat wine and food as separate policy spheres.

And wine has, well, come off the agenda.

The reasons for this probably have to do with the supermarket lobby, the difficulty in harmonising some of the priorities of the peak industry body, and the peculiar way that the industry has evolved, as a result of both domestic and foreign pressures, after 2021.

This article will be the first of several for Grapegrower & Winemaker that will attempt to explain the predicament that the Australian wine industry currently finds itself in.

We are in the position we are because of irrelevant and insufficiently robust legislation when it comes to anti-competitive behaviour in both the buying and the selling side of the Australian wine industry.

So we are all going to be spooked by ghosts for another few years at least.

It would only take a bit of coordinated action for the industry itself to vanquish them. But it lacks both the cohesiveness and the collective spirit to achieve that on its own terms. It has been far too kind on the supermarkets.

This needs to change. There are innovative policy ways to achieve reform, but it will involve rethinking a lot of presumptions about the industry.

In the meantime, we remain at the whims of a parliament that cares more about food than drink, cares more about beer than wine, and cares more about alcohol abuse than it does about unfair bargaining, duplicitous labelling, and, more generally, the concentration of power in the buying and selling spheres of the grape and wine sector.

 

References

¹ Evan Jones, ‘Liquor retailing and the Woolworths/Coles juggernaut’, Journal of Australian Political Economy (2005) 55: 23-47.

² Edward Cavanagh, ‘The ghost of John Riddoch’, Substack (27 May 2026), available at: http://ewcavanagh.substack.com/p/the-ghost-of-john-riddoch (viewed 31 May 2026).

3 Once a small-scale traditional-method sparkling house label near Ballarat, Yellowglen was acquired by Mildara in 1984 for $2 million, whereupon it was transformed into a ubiquitous brand for cheap, mass-produced, tank-finished, and regionally unspecific fizz. See Ray King, King’s Run: A maverick’s tale of football, wine, and business (Glen Waverly: Sid Harta, 2015), 348.

⁴ ‘Woolies refining own label strategy’, The Shout (1 March 2013), available at: http://www.theshout.com.au/woolies-refining-own-label-strategy/ (accessed 31 May 2026).

⁵ Edward Cavanagh, ‘Where did the Australian “cellar door” come from?’, Substack (19 October 2025), available at: http://ewcavanagh.substack.com/p/where-did-the-australian-cellar-door (viewed 31 May 2026).

title: Marlborough Young Winemaker of the Year for 2026 has been crowned
summary:
Nick Lamain from Isabel Estate has been crowned the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Marlborough Young Winemaker of the Year, following the competition held yesterday at the Marlborough Research Centre (MRC) and Nelson Marlborough Institute of Technology (NMIT) in Blenheim. “I’m thrilled,” said Lamain, who had been studying hard in the build-up to the competition, determined to win. He also competed in the 2024 National Final and hopes his previous experience will stand him in good stead for this year’s finals. New Zealand Winegrowers also extended congratulations to Katasha Pearce from Saint Clair Family Estate, who came second, and to Paiqi Cao from Indevin, who came third. The organisation said that there were ten contestants total, including: O'Callaghan, Marisco; Briahana Adams, Constellation Brands; Erina Lim, Framingham; Jamie Howe, Nautilus Estate; Juan Bautista Fernandez, Te Pa; Michael Hix, Yealands; and Zoe Holcroft, Rose Family Estate.

The post Marlborough Young Winemaker of the Year for 2026 has been crowned appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
New Zealand Winegrowers said there were ten contestants total, making for a tough yet well-spirited competition. Images supplied

Nick Lamain from Isabel Estate has been crowned the 2026 Tonnellerie de Mercurey Marlborough Young Winemaker of the Year, following the competition held yesterday at the Marlborough Research Centre (MRC) and Nelson Marlborough Institute of Technology (NMIT) in Blenheim.

“I’m thrilled,” said Lamain, who had been studying hard in the build-up to the competition, determined to win.

He also competed in the 2024 National Final and hopes his previous experience will stand him in good stead for this year’s finals.

Dave Tyney, representing Tonnellerie de Mercurey, presented him with the trophy.

New Zealand Winegrowers also extended congratulations to Katasha Pearce from Saint Clair Family Estate, who came second, and to Paiqi Cao from Indevin, who came third.

Katasha Pearce, Nick Lamain and Paiqi Cao.

Lamain won a Fruitfed Supplies Field Trip and $1,000 cash. Pearce won $750 cash and a New Zealand Society for Viticulture and Oenology (NZSVO) Educational Trip for coming second, and Cao won $500 cash for coming third.

Lamain will now represent Marlborough in the National Final at Foley’s The Runholder in Martinborough on 25 August.

The ten contestants for the 2026 Marlborough Young Winemakers competition.

The organisation said that there were ten contestants total, including: O’Callaghan, Marisco; Briahana Adams, Constellation Brands; Erina Lim, Framingham; Jamie Howe, Nautilus Estate; Juan Bautista Fernandez, Te Pa; Michael Hix, Yealands; and Zoe Holcroft, Rose Family Estate.

This reportedly made for “a tough competition”, however, New Zealand Winegrowers said there was “a really positive vibe” amongst the group as they cheered one another on.

The contestants worked their way through a variety of challenges, including blending, laboratory work, wine judging, presentations, an interview and general wine industry knowledge.

The Constellation Brands Laboratory section was taken out by Jamie Howe and the Indevin Wine Judging section by Nick Lamain. Jamie Howe also won the best Fruitfed Supplies speech, “fighting off stiff competition as all the contestants delivered speeches of a very high standard”.

Katasha Pearce won the People’s Choice for her rosé blend, as voted by the dinner guests who tasted all the contestants’ blends blind.

The awards dinner was held at the Marlborough Conference Centre with Brian Bicknell from Mahi Wines as MC. Kelly Stuart from Cloudy Bay, chair of the Marlborough Young Winemaker Committee, also acknowledged the high calibre of the contestants.

The post Marlborough Young Winemaker of the Year for 2026 has been crowned appeared first on Winetitles.

title: Mornington Peninsula’s VineHop Festival receives government support
summary:
VineHop Festival has been announced as a recipient of funding through the Victorian Government’s Regional Events Fund, bringing with it a “powerful vote of confidence in the [Mornington] Peninsula's thriving events scene and the extraordinary community of makers and producers who call this region home”. On 21 November 2026 festival-goers will make their way between wineries, cideries and breweries to hear live music, enjoy food and “the very best” drinks the Peninsula has to offer. This year's event will feature headline acts Eric Powell, Superdisco Club and Andy Murphy with a target crowd of 6,500 people. Minister for Tourism, Natalie Suleyman, said the government is delighted to support VineHop through the fund.

The post Mornington Peninsula’s VineHop Festival receives government support appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
The festival has received funding through the Victorian Government’s Regional Events Fund. Image supplied

VineHop Festival has been announced as a recipient of funding through the Victorian Government’s Regional Events Fund, bringing with it a “powerful vote of confidence in the [Mornington] Peninsula’s thriving events scene and the extraordinary community of makers and producers who call this region home”.

On 21 November 2026 festival-goers will make their way between wineries, cideries and breweries to hear live music, enjoy food and “the very best” drinks the Peninsula has to offer.

This year’s event will feature headline acts Eric Powell, Superdisco Club and Andy Murphy with a target crowd of 6,500 people.

Minister for Tourism, Natalie Suleyman, said the government is delighted to support VineHop through the fund.

“Events like this provide another fantastic reason for visitors to experience the Mornington Peninsula region – in turn supporting local jobs and businesses,” Suleyman said.

Organisers said that in 2025 VineHop welcomed 5,445 attendees, “generating $1.76 million in economic impact for the local region and 1,800 overnight stays”. Nearly half of all attendees travelled from outside the Peninsula, with Melbourne suburbs accounting for 35% of the crowd. Festival-goers reportedly spent an average of $153.60 at the event itself and a further $105.66 exploring the wider region on the day.

Furthermore, event runners claim that 71% of VineHop attendees are aged 35 to 49 and 84% are parents. 93% said they would recommend VineHop to a friend and 92% planned to return the following year.

Festival Director and Mac Events co-founder Lisa MacGregor said that the Mornington Peninsula is “something really special” and VineHop exists to share that with as many people as possible.

“The support of the Victorian Government through Visit Victoria means we can do that on an even bigger scale in 2026. We get to shine a light on our local winemakers, brewers, distillers and cider makers and bring thousands of people to this region who may never have visited before.

“Every year we see people fall in love with the Peninsula at VineHop. They come for the festival, and they come back for everything else. That’s what this event is really about.”

Tickets are on sale now online via the VineHop event website.

title: Renowned winemaker reveals new focus on namesake brand following departure from Endeavour
summary:
Endeavour Group has confirmed that David Bicknell has departed from his position as chief winemaker at Oakridge Wines in the Yarra Valley. Daily Wine News reached out to Bicknell to discuss his future plans. “I have a small family winemaking business, Bicknell FC, which we started in 2011. So far, it’s been a bit of a busman’s holiday as I was completely consumed at Oakridge. The only current plan is to concentrate on developing that business, although I will be happy to talk anyone interested in my services,” he shared. Bicknell’s wines can be purchased directly via his website or through its distributor Real Wines. David Bicknell led winemaking at Oakridge Wines for nearly 25 years, beginning his time there in 2002. Under his leadership, Oakridge was awarded the number one winery in Australia by both the Halliday Wine Companion and The Real Review in 2023. He announced his departure via a post to Instagram on Monday.

The post Renowned winemaker reveals new focus on namesake brand following departure from Endeavour appeared first on Winetitles.
content:
David Bicknell is willing to speak with anyone interested in his services and will focus on his own wine brand. Image supplied

By Kyra Trussell

Endeavour Group has confirmed that David Bicknell has departed from his position as chief winemaker at Oakridge Wines in the Yarra Valley.

Daily Wine News reached out to Bicknell to discuss his future plans.

“I have a small family winemaking business, Bicknell FC, which we started in 2011. So far, it’s been a bit of a busman’s holiday as I was completely consumed at Oakridge. The only current plan is to concentrate on developing that business, although I will be happy to talk anyone interested in my services,” he shared.

Bicknell’s wines can be purchased directly via his website or through its distributor Real Wines.

David Bicknell led winemaking at Oakridge Wines for nearly 25 years, beginning his time there in 2002. Under his leadership, Oakridge was awarded the number one winery in Australia by both the Halliday Wine Companion and The Real Review in 2023.

He announced his departure via a post to Instagram on Monday.

“It’s been an amazing ride, taking what was broken winery to the top of the industry with an unwavering commitment to quality and plenty of hard graft. Some really good times with some really good people.

“They say you never know you’re going to get boned until you do. And so, it is. Thankfully, I leave a highly trained, capable and talented team for the next piece of history. Your support for them would mean a lot to me. Love my team,” he wrote.

As reported by The Australian, Bicknell does not wish to comment further on the departure and told the outlet simply, “I did not choose to leave Oakridge”.

Speaking to Daily Wine News, a spokesperson for Endeavour Group commented on Bicknell’s departure and the future of leadership at Oakridge.

“David Bicknell has departed Oakridge following more than 20 years of service at the winery. He will be succeeded by senior winemaker, Tim Dexter.

“Tim has worked with David since 2023 and will continue the close partnership with award-winning viticulturist Steve Faulkner.

“David helped build Oakridge into the brand it is today while mentoring the next generation of winemakers who will carry the winery forward.

“The Oakridge team and operations are continuing business as usual,” they said.

Michael Fragos, chief winemaker at Chapel Hill in McLaren Vale, has also departed from his position.

The spokesperson for Endeavour thanked Fragos for his time at Chapel Hill.

“Michael Fragos has made a valuable contribution to Chapel Hill. Endeavour Group thanks Michael for his service and dedication.”

Michael Fragos began working at Chapel Hill as senior winemaker in 2004 and was eventually appointed to the position of chief winemaker in 2013. Notably, Fragos received the title of ‘Winemaker of the Year’ at the London International Wine & Spirit Competition (IWSC) in 2007.

Fragos has not commented publicly on his departure as of the publication of this article.

Endeavour recently appointed Colliers for the sale of Oakridge Wines and Elders for the sale of Chapel Hill.
